project_description.text: The U.S. EPA's Office of Noise Abatement and Control proposes federal noise emission regulations for newly manufactured street and off-road motorcycles, mopeds, and motorcycle replacement exhaust systems under the Noise Control Act. The rule sets a phased standard reaching 78 dB(A) by 1985 for street and small off-road motorcycles, 82 dB(A) by 1983 for larger off-road motorcycles, and 70 dB(A) for mopeds, measured under newly developed test procedures. It also establishes a one-year Acoustical Assurance Period, labeling requirements, and an enforcement program covering production verification, selective audits, and in-use compliance.

affected_community.text: The rule affects urban, suburban, and rural residents exposed to motorcycle noise—an estimated 1.7 million daily outdoor speech-interference events from street motorcycles and roughly 1–2 million people impacted daily by off-road motorcycles across more than 33,000 square miles. Motorcycle operators and passengers themselves face ear-level sound exposures near 100–105 dB(A), posing hearing-loss risks. Industry stakeholders include major Japanese manufacturers, AMF/Harley-Davidson (with about 3,000 jobs in Milwaukee and York, PA), smaller European and U.S. makers, over 100 aftermarket exhaust firms, and roughly 7,000–8,000 franchised dealers, along with state and local enforcement officials and federal land managers.

alternatives_overview.text: EPA evaluated taking no action, requiring labeling only, or setting standards at study levels of 86, 83, 80, 78, or 75 dB(A), with parallel options for off-road motorcycles and a two-class split at 170 cc, plus normal versus accelerated lead times from 1979 to 1991. Replacement exhaust systems and competition motorcycles were each considered for full regulation versus labeling only, and non-EPA strategies such as sales limits and pollution taxes were also reviewed. EPA rejected 75 dB(A) as not achievable with best available technology and 80 dB(A) as insufficient, selecting 78 dB(A) for street motorcycles, 82 dB(A) for large off-road motorcycles, and labeling only for competition models.

environmental_impact.text: The proposed standards are projected to reduce street motorcycle outdoor speech interference by 55–75% and sleep disturbance by 50–65%, and to cut noise-impacted off-road area by 25–35% (about 80,000–115,000 square miles). Side effects include a 5–10% fuel economy penalty (10–20 million extra gallons per year), possible power loss up to 15%, and modest weight increases, while water quality, solid waste, and air emissions impacts are minor and wildlife may benefit slightly. Motorcycle prices are expected to rise 7–10%, with annualized industry costs near $200 million and possible loss of 3,000–5,000 jobs relative to baseline.

public_response.text: EPA conducted extensive outreach with every state, numerous local governments, environmental and user groups, manufacturers, dealers, and federal agencies including the Forest Service, BLM, and DOT, and distributed advance copies of the draft rule for comment. State and local officials broadly urged EPA to set federal standards, citing difficulty enforcing in-use and land-use restrictions, while attitudinal surveys consistently ranked motorcycles as a top noise annoyance and federal land managers identified trail motorcycles as their most urgent off-road recreational vehicle problem. Some European and specialty manufacturers warned they might withdraw from the U.S. market at stricter levels, and aftermarket exhaust firms raised concerns about testing and labeling burdens.

value.name: Federal New Product Noise Standard
value.description: Establishment of a Federal noise emission standard for new motorcycles, setting not-to-exceed sound level limits measured by the F-76a test procedure. Multiple regulatory study levels were analyzed (75, 78, 80, and 83 dB(A)) with various effective dates.

value.name: Alternatives to Federal Regulation
value.description: Various non-Federal approaches to controlling motorcycle noise, discussed in Section 10 of the document, including reliance on state, local, and other non-Federal mechanisms rather than a Federal new product standard.

value.name: No Action (No Federal Standard)
value.description: Continuation of current unregulated conditions for new motorcycle noise emissions at the Federal level, serving as the baseline against which regulatory options are compared. Under this scenario, motorcycle sound levels and the proportion of exhaust-modified motorcycles remain unchanged.

themes.justification: The document centers on EPA federal noise emission rulemaking under the Noise Control Act—directly reflecting 'Environmental Regulation and Policy'—targeting newly manufactured motorcycles and mopeds as a transportation category, with impacts on urban/suburban/rural mobility corridors and off-road landscapes spanning over 33,000 square miles.

geocoded.name: Washington, D.C.

location.note: This is a nationwide federal rulemaking EIS for motorcycle noise emission standards, not a geographically specific infrastructure or development project. Washington D.C. is the issuing agency location. No discrete project area exists.
